column1: SECTION: CATH LAB INDICATIONS & PRIORITY ORDER

column9: Definition
Acute Coronary Syndrome (unstable angina, NSTEMI or STEMI) is ≤ 24 hours prior to cath lab presentation. Cannot be selected with ACS > 24 hrs.
Acute Coronary Syndrome (unstable angina, NSTEMI or STEMI) is >24 hours prior to cath lab presentation (STEMI/NSTEMI ≤7 days from symptoms). Cannot be selected with ACS ≤ 24 hrs.
The patient is experiencing new onset angina within the last 2 months with no prior history of angina, MI, PCI, or CABG.
The patient is stable (no ACS signs/symptoms, no new/worsening angina, no hemodynamic instability) and has known coronary artery disease ≥50% in at least one vessel.
Suspected Coronary Artery Disease, no prior documentation of CAD ≥50% in a vessel.
The patient has a history of angina that has changed in frequency, pattern or severity over the past 2 months.
The patient presents status post cardiac arrest.
There is disease of at least one heart valve.
Pericardial disease is inflammation of the pericardial sac.
Cardiac arrhythmia is also known as cardiac dysrhythmia or irregular heartbeat — heartbeat is irregular, too fast, or too slow.
Cardiomyopathy is a disease of the heart muscle (e.g. hypertrophic, dilated, restrictive, arrhythmogenic RV dysplasia, Takotsubo).
In left ventricular heart failure, the left side of the heart must work harder to pump the same amount of blood. Best fit for Heart Failure patients.
Abrupt, transient, complete loss of consciousness with inability to maintain postural tone and rapid spontaneous recovery.
A cardiac transplant is a heart transplanted from a donor.
Cardiac evaluation of the coronary arteries and/or LV function.
The patient presents for clearance to participate in an exercise program or cardiac rehab.
Use only when no other Cath Lab indications apply to the patient's situation.
